summary: Evans explores why knowledge gets trapped in individuals' heads at work and how teams can spread expertise more broadly. She identifies discovery as a major problem — knowing who knows what is surprisingly difficult even among close colleagues. She examines why system builders become permanent experts and why others struggle to develop the same depth, pointing to historical context and a sense of entitlement to understanding. She proposes several practical solutions: directing questions to new people to build them into 'caches' of knowledge, apprenticeship models, internal talks, and the magical tactic of asking 'how did you find that out?' after someone solves a problem.
key_points: Discovery is a major barrier: people often have no idea what expertise their coworkers hold, from previous professional experience to open source projects to self-study
The 'person builds system, person is expert forever' pattern is the default and it's harmful — it creates bus factors and prevents others from developing expertise
Answering questions is itself an investment in expertise — putting someone in a question-answering rotation forces them to build and verify their understanding
The One True Expert must sometimes deliberately hold back from answering, even when it would be faster, to let others develop
Asking 'how did you find that out?' immediately after someone solves a problem is one of the most effective ways to extract tacit knowledge
money_quotes: There are a bajillion people who know amazing things, and often that knowledge gets stuck in their head and other people don't end up learning it. For years, sometimes!
I kind of feel like telling someone that they can't understand a system or that it's 'too hard' is like… a crime. We should all expect to be able to understand the software systems that we work with!
You can build people who are 'caches' for information – put them in charge of answering questions, they slowly start to accumulate knowledge and eventually become experts.
If everyone always asks Person A questions about the System, then that is itself an organizational investment in Person A's knowledge.
The One True Expert has a role here too – they need to sometimes hold off on answering questions and doing debugging work, even if it would be faster for them to do it.
This is a MAGICAL TACTIC: 'how did you find that out?' Often experts don't realize what they know, but if you ask them what they did FIVE MINUTES AFTER THEY DID IT they can usually remember.
I basically think this is a travesty. People occasionally run internal talks about topics they know about which is THE BEST.
key_insight: Knowledge gets locked in people's heads not because they're unwilling to share, but because discovery of who knows what is broken, and organizations default to single experts instead of deliberately investing in building new ones through question-routing, apprenticeships, and extracting tacit knowledge.
